scenarios.title: signs in with valid credentials @positive
scenarios.userAction: Navigate to /login, fill email + password with valid creds, click Sign in, dashboard greeting visible.

scenarios.title: rejects an invalid password @negative
scenarios.userAction: Navigate to /login, fill valid email + wrong password, click Sign in, error banner shows invalid credentials.

locatorTable.notes: Id `email` strongly suggests an email input, and the form convention implies a `<label>` for `email`, but no DOM snapshot confirms the label text. Preserve `page.locator('#email')` as fallback if label is absent — see Q2.
Same rationale as `#email`. Standard login forms universally label the password field; no DOM snapshot to confirm — see Q2.

The element is used with `.click()` in a form-submission context, making a `<button>` or `<input type="submit">` likely. However `getByText` matches any element — could be a styled `<a>` or `<div>`. **Review needed: confirm element tag before upgrading to `getByRole`.** Fallback: `page.getByText('Sign in', { exact: true })` (HIGH confidence, keeps current semantics) — see Q5.

CSS class only; no DOM evidence of role, `aria-label`, or `data-testid`. Cannot propose `getByRole` or `getByText` without knowing the element tag and runtime text. Reviewer should add a `data-testid="dashboard-greeting"` to the component or confirm a heading role — see Q3.

Error notifications frequently carry `role="alert"` in accessible implementations; `.error-banner` class name reinforces this guess. If the element lacks a role, fallback is `page.locator('.error-banner')` (LOW). If `.error-banner` matches multiple elements on the page, scope with `.filter({ hasText: /invalid credentials/i })` — see Q4.
